messages.content: a thing you've kept

My grandfather's pocket watch. The crystal cracked years ago. It still runs five minutes fast. I wind it every Sunday morning. I haven't checked the time on it once in eight years. It's the winding I want.
something you've kept that you can't explain

A rock from the beach in Galveston. April 2016. Will picked it up. Said it looked like a face. He gave it to me. It's gray with a darker streak. I keep it in the drawer with the receipts I never throw out.
an object that means a lot
My grandmother's wedding ring. She gave it to me when I was twelve, before her mind went. I don't wear it. I keep it in a small box on the dresser. Some weeks I open the box just to see it.
something irreplaceable
A box of letters from Sam in Berlin. 2019, before everything that came after. Six letters. Hand-written. The fourth one is the one I read first when I'm having a bad week.
a possession that holds memory
My dad's old Walkman from the eighties. The headphones are gone. The cassettes inside are blank tapes he made for himself. I haven't listened to them. I keep them in the desk drawer.
something you've held onto for years
A high school ID from junior year. The photo is terrible. I keep it because my hair was an experiment that semester. My mom thought it was beautiful. I didn't believe her at the time.
an object you can't throw away
A silver bracelet my grandmother gave me at thirteen. The clasp is broken. It would cost twelve dollars to fix. I haven't done it in twenty years.
a tangible thing from someone who's gone
My uncle's flannel shirt. He died in 2008. I wear it on cold days. It still smells like his garage even though that doesn't make sense.
a small thing that matters
A pebble I found on the path to my grandmother's house when I was eight. Smooth, gray, almost translucent. It lives in the small bowl on my desk.
a book you've never been able to give away
My mother's copy of A Wrinkle in Time. The spine is broken. There's a coffee ring on page 47. Her name is in the front in careful nine-year-old handwriting. I won't give it away.
a piece of clothing with weight
My father's gray cardigan from when I was small. He wore it every cold morning. He gave it to me at twenty-three. The cuffs are worn through. I patched them once badly.
a thing you keep for a person
Will Anderson's pocket knife. He gave it to me the second time I visited the hospital. I've never opened the blade. It's in the drawer with the letters.
a kitchen object with memory
My grandmother's Pyrex bowl. The blue one with the white pattern. She made bread in it for sixty years. I make bread in it now. The bottom has a hairline crack.
a piece of paper you can't recycle
A grocery list my mother wrote in 1996. I found it in a coat pocket years later. Bread, milk, oranges, a name I don't recognize. I keep it in the desk.
something stupid you've kept
A ticket stub from a movie I went to with Sam in 2018. The Worst Person in the World. We didn't talk for six months after that night. I still have the stub.
a thing that means more than it should
A hand-painted come-in sign from Devon. Cracked acrylic on plywood. Used to hang at the studio. We replaced it last year. I took the old one home and put it in the garage.
something you've inherited that you weren't supposed to
A small notebook of my grandmother's. Recipes. Some prayers. A list of the names of the cats she had. It was supposed to go to my aunt. It didn't.
a treasured ordinary object
A blue mug from the diner I worked at in 2009. I took it the day I quit. It chipped the next year. It's the mug I drink from when I write before dawn.
a notebook you've kept
A leather one from the trip to Edinburgh in 2017. The first half is dense. The last twenty pages are blank. I've never been able to write in them.
something that follows you between houses
A small black-and-white photograph of my grandmother in her thirties. She's standing in front of a house I never saw. It's hung above the desk in every place I've lived since college.
a piece of jewelry
The thin gold chain my mother wore when I was a child. She gave it to me in 2019. I wear it on the days that need it. The clasp is delicate. I've nearly lost it twice.
a thing in your closet
A green wool blanket my grandmother made. The middle stripe got stained. She fixed it. I sleep under it on cold nights. It's older than me.
something tactile you reach for
A smooth wooden spoon. My grandmother used it. The handle is worn into the shape of a hand. I make every soup with it.
a piece of furniture with history
The kitchen table. My parents' first piece. They bought it in 1980 in Granbury for forty dollars. It came to me when I moved into my own house. It's pine. I sit at it every morning.
a thing on your nightstand
A small bell my grandmother used to ring when she wanted me to come help in the kitchen. I haven't rung it in twenty-seven years. I keep it next to the lamp.
a child's drawing you kept
My nephew Theo's drawing of a tree with seven cats in it. He was three. Daniel sent it. It's on the fridge. It's been there a year.
something a friend gave you
A small ceramic pot Naomi made in 2018 in a beginner's class. It's slightly off-balance. She tried to give it to me as a joke. I kept it. I put pens in it.
something stolen from somewhere
A spoon from a hotel in Edinburgh. Junior year semester. I don't know why I took it. I drink coffee with it in the morning.
a memento you don't talk about
A page torn from a book Will gave me. He marked it with his thumb when we read together at the hospital. I never put the page back. It lives in a folder.
the smallest meaningful thing you own
A button. From my grandfather's coat. I found it in the lining when I was sixteen, going through the closet after his funeral. I've kept it in my wallet for twenty years.
a thing you almost lost
My father's pen. Sterling silver. The cap nearly fell off in the airport security tray in 2014. I'd had it for nine years by then. I'd wept if I'd lost it.
a thing you carry with you
A small index card with my mother's handwriting. A line from a poem I don't know the rest of. I've carried it for eight years.
a thing too small to be important
A pressed leaf from the maple in front of my parents' house. I picked it up when I was eight. It's flat and brown now. It's in my third notebook on the third shelf.
a clock or watch with story
My grandfather's pocket watch. He carried it for forty years. He gave it to me when he was dying. It still gains five minutes a day. I wind it every Sunday.
a piece of art you've kept
A small ink drawing by Sam from 2019. A bird I can't identify. He sent it from Berlin in the second-to-last letter. It's framed above my desk.
someone you've lost

Will Anderson. 2021. He was twenty-five. I drove to Houston three times. The third time he was already mostly gone. I sat with him for nine hours. His mother came in around two in the morning. She thanked me for being there. I didn't know what to say. I still don't.
who you've lost recently
My uncle Dan. December 2023. He had been sick for three years. I drove to Granbury for the funeral. My mother sang at it. I hadn't heard her sing since I was eight.
a person you grieve quietly
My grandfather. 2003. I was thirteen. He taught me to make sourdough on Saturdays. I make it now and feel him at the elbow. Twenty-two years of loaves and the feeling has not gotten quieter.
a friend you've lost contact with

Sam. Berlin. After the last letter in 2019, three months of nothing. Then a postcard with no return address. Then nothing. I think about him on the second Saturday of every month. I don't know why that day.
a person you've lost who's still alive
My grandmother. Memory care since 2018. The body is here. The person is mostly not. I visit every six weeks. She doesn't know me anymore. I sit with her anyway. I read her poems she used to read me.
someone you'd want back for an hour
My grandfather. I'd want to ask him about the war. He never spoke about it. I have three sentences I remember him saying about it across thirteen years.
a person who's gone and you still write to
Will. Letters I don't send. I've written eleven of them since 2021. They're in a folder labeled with his name. I've never read them back.
a death that changed something

My grandmother's brother. 2010. I'd never met him. He left her a small inheritance she gave to me to start the move to Austin. I would not have come here without that money. He shaped my life from a distance, after his death.
a clean ending

Sam, March 2019. We sat in his apartment in Berlin. Last night before my flight. We didn't pretend the romantic thing wasn't over. We didn't make it ugly. We had soup. We talked about a book we both loved. Hugged at the door. Both knew. Neither performed it.
a goodbye that worked

My grandfather. 2003. He was lucid for three days at the end. He held my hand on the second day and said my name. We talked about the bread starter. He told me it would always work if I fed it. We both knew. Neither cried.
a relationship that ended well

Marcus, 2012. We dated for two years. We had outgrown each other. We sat on his couch in October. Talked about it for two hours. Cried. Laughed. Each of us made the other a list of three things we'd loved. He kept mine on his fridge for a year.
the kind of ending that doesn't haunt you
Quitting the diner job in 2009. I'd worked there four years. The owner cried. I cried. We had coffee together at the end of my last shift. He told me to write the next chapter. I have.
a moment of release

Selling my parents' house in Granbury after my dad's stroke. Eleanor moved into the smaller place in town. We sat on the porch the last morning. Drank coffee. Said one sentence each about the house. Got in the car.
a peaceful break

With my college roommate after graduation. We knew we wouldn't keep up. We didn't pretend. The last week we cooked together every night. The last morning we hugged for a long time. We've exchanged Christmas cards for fifteen years.
an ending without bitterness

My therapist before this one. We worked for four years. I knew it had to end. I told her in March. We had three more sessions to close. The last one was an hour of me thanking her in fragments and her listening.
a goodbye to a place

My first apartment in Austin. 2010. Six hundred square feet. I had grown up there. The morning I left I made coffee and sat on the floor with my back against the wall. Cried for ten minutes. Locked the door.
a clean cut you didn't expect to be clean
Leaving the church I grew up in. 2013. The minister didn't try to talk me out of it. He shook my hand and said I always had a place. I'm not religious now. I still send him a card at Christmas.
a relationship you ended kindly

Rae, May 2015. We had been dating for three months. It wasn't going to work. I drove to her place. We sat at her kitchen table. I said the truth. She said it back. We hugged. She closed the door behind me. I wept in the car.
the way you said goodbye to your grandmother before she forgot

December 2017. The week before she went into care. She still knew me. I sat at her kitchen table. We didn't talk much. She held my hand for a long time. She said: be brave with what you've got. I said: I'll try. Both of us knew that was the last lucid one.
a closing without drama

Closing the writers group in 2019. It had run its course. We met for a final meeting. Each person read one piece. We had wine. Diego made a toast that was mostly a sentence. We agreed to see each other anyway.
first time you remember music in the house

Nine years old. The Yamaha upright arrived. My dad sat in front of it for an hour with his hands in his lap. Played the next morning, before breakfast. Bill Evans. The house had had a piano-shaped quiet for as long as I'd been alive. Then it didn't.
first poem you remember

Mary Oliver, ninth grade. My English teacher gave me Devotions. I read Wild Geese at lunch in the cafeteria. I sat with it for the rest of the period. I copied two stanzas onto the inside cover of my notebook. I kept the notebook.
first time you wrote something that worked

Eleventh grade. I wrote about my grandmother's hands. The teacher read it out loud to the class without my permission. I was furious. Then I realized it had landed. The fury and the realization at the same time, in the same minute.
first time you stayed up writing all night

Sophomore year of college. The kitchen of the apartment on 30th. I drank too much coffee. Wrote a single poem about my mother that took until five in the morning. I still have it. It is not a good poem. It was a real one.
first job that taught you something

The diner. Sixteen. I learned that you don't get tipped for the right things. I learned that the regulars want to be remembered. I learned that I am not built for hospitality work. I lasted a year and a half.
first time you read at a reading

Ten people in a coffee shop on Manor in 2009. I read three poems. My voice shook for the first one. The second one was steadier. The third I forgot a stanza and improvised the rest. Naomi was in the front row. She drove me home.
first heartbreak

Marcus, 2012. He didn't break my heart. The end of the relationship did. I wept for three days. Didn't go to work. My roommate brought me soup. I lost five pounds. The fourth morning I wrote about it. The writing was the beginning of being okay.
first thing you remember about your grandmother
Her hands. Veined, sun-spotted. She was making bread when I was four. She let me push my fingers into the dough. She didn't tell me how. She let me figure it out from the resistance.
first time you went to a funeral
My grandfather's. Thirteen. I sat in the second row next to my brother. I didn't cry. Daniel did. I held his hand. I learned that day that grief sometimes shows up in the wrong order.
first time you knew you were a writer

Twenty-one. Junior year of college. Edinburgh. Walking along the Water of Leith in the rain. A line came into my head. I stopped on the path and wrote it on a receipt. Knew, in that minute, this is what I do. The line was not particularly good. The knowing was.
first time you took yourself seriously

Twenty-five. The morning after a reading where another writer had told me my poems were the strongest in the room. I had not believed her until I woke up the next day and the words were still there. I made coffee. Sat at the desk. Wrote for two hours. Hadn't done that before.
first time you understood your father

After the stroke. He sat at the piano with his hands in his lap, the way he had at thirty when the Yamaha first arrived. Same gesture. Different cause. I understood, that morning, what loving an instrument means. And losing it.
first place you lived alone

The 600-square-foot apartment in Austin. 2009. Hyde Park. The radiator banged. I could touch both walls of the kitchen with my arms out. I lived there four years. I learned how to live alone. I learned what books I needed in arm's reach.
first time you made bread
My grandmother's kitchen. Five. She let me push my hands into dough. I thought it was alive. I asked. She said yes, in a way.
first sermon that landed
My uncle's funeral. December 2023. The minister read from Ecclesiastes. The word time. Used eleven times. I counted. It was the only sermon that has ever held still for me.
first time you knew you could write through pain

After Will. Six weeks after. I had not written anything since. One morning at five I sat at the desk and the page didn't refuse me. The poem was bad. The fact that I could put words next to each other again was not.
first time you held your nephew
Theo at eight days. Portland. I cried when I held him. Daniel was making coffee. He came over and put his hand on my shoulder. He didn't say anything. He didn't need to.
first kiss with someone you were going to keep

Marcus, junior year of college. On the porch of the house off Guadalupe. The light was the kind of late September light that makes everything sound expensive. We were both shaking. He laughed first. I laughed second. Then we got serious.
first time someone asked you to teach

Threshold, 2019. Maya asked. I said no for a week. Then yes. The first session I had four kids. I read them Mary Oliver and asked them to write what they noticed in the room. One of them wrote about the radiator. That was the moment.
first room you remember

My grandmother's spare bedroom. Ages two through six, weekends. The wallpaper had small blue flowers. The quilt was green and yellow. I learned to read in that room. I learned to be quiet without being lonely.
first time you lied to your mother and got away with it

Eleven. About where I had been after school. She knew. She didn't say so. I learned, in that minute, that adults sometimes choose silence. I respected her more for it. I did not lie to her again for a long time.
first morning of a new chapter

The first morning at Threshold, March 2020. The lockdown two weeks away. I didn't know it yet. I made coffee. Walked to the office. Met Maya in the parking lot. Knew the work was right within the hour.
a place that taught you something

Edinburgh. October 2017. Junior year semester. Walking along the Water of Leith in the rain. The first time I understood that walking was a way of thinking. I had read Wordsworth on it but I had not lived it. That semester I wrote eleven poems. Two of them are still in the chapbook.
a city that changed you

Berlin. Spring 2019, three weeks visiting Sam. The cold made me write. The architecture made me notice. The walks at midnight along Spree taught me about the relationship between silence and language. I came back different.
a landscape that taught you patience

The Davis Mountains. 2015. A weekend alone at a cabin. I had brought a notebook and three books. I read none of them. I sat on the porch for hours. Learned that some thinking has to wait until the mind has been bored for long enough.
a place you can't go back to

My grandmother's house in Granbury. Sold in 2010 after she went into care. New owners painted it gray. The dogwood is gone. Some places you carry with you because they no longer exist for anyone else to see.
a town that taught you you were not from there

New York, 2012. Visiting Maddy for two weeks. The pace was wrong for my body. I sat in a coffee shop on Bedford and could feel the city moving around me at the wrong speed. I went home and was grateful for the slowness.
a place that wasn't beautiful but taught you something

The Houston ICU waiting room. 2021. Visiting Will. The vending machine. The fluorescent light. The chairs that did not let you sleep. I learned what a vigil is. I learned that there is no comfortable way to wait.
a room that shaped you

My grandmother's kitchen. Through age twelve. Saturday mornings. The bread board. The window over the sink. The blue tile she had put in herself. The first place I understood that work and love sit in the same room.
a place where you became more yourself

The first apartment alone in Austin. Hyde Park. Four years. I learned how I lived when no one was watching. I learned how to be alone without being lonely. I learned what books I needed in arm's reach.
a body of water that's stayed with you

The Brazos near Granbury. Walked it with my grandfather when I was small. Walked it with my mother after my dad's stroke. Will be walking it again when I go home. It has held the same five generations.
a place where you learned to be quiet
The cabin in the Davis Mountains. The hours on the porch. No phone signal. No one to perform for. The wind in the live oaks. I learned what my voice sounded like without an audience.
a place you went to grieve

The bench by the lake at Town Lake. After Will. October 2021. I went there every morning for two weeks. Sat in the cold. Wrote nothing. Watched the herons. The grief had a shape and I needed a place that could hold it.
a place that was the wrong place

A studio in Brooklyn for three months in 2014. I had thought I would stay. I learned in the first month that I would not. The light was wrong. The street was wrong. The kind of attention I had to give to live there was a kind I did not want to develop.
a country that humbled you

Scotland. The semester in Edinburgh. I had thought of it as a study abroad. It taught me that I had a tourist's American confidence I had not known I was carrying. The slowness, the directness, the lack of small talk. I was disabused.
a place where you finally understood your grandmother

The garden at Granbury. The May after she went into care. I weeded the row of zinnias she had planted. I understood, with my hands in the dirt, why she had said the same things every May for forty years. The garden taught me what she was.
a place you learned a language has limits

Berlin, evening, walking with Sam. He spoke German with someone on the corner. I understood nothing. Came home and wrote about the gap. About what it means to live in language and what it means to live without it. Three of the chapbook poems came from that walk.
a kitchen that became a teacher

Naomi's first apartment kitchen. Cramped. The stove tilted. We made one terrible curry there in 2015 that became the joke for nine years. The kitchen taught me how to laugh at a friend's failure without diminishing her.
a place where you proved something to yourself

The first reading I gave in Houston. 2015. I drove there alone. Read three poems to twelve people. Drove back the same night. Five hours each way. I had not believed I could do that. I had been wrong.
a place where the silence was instructive

My father's kitchen, after the stroke. The silence of a house where the music had stopped. I sat at the table while he sat in the chair. The silence taught me something about what loss does to a room. I have written about it three times since.
